1: A theme: "What shall we say, then? Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase?" The antinomian objection to grace is addressed. The question shows that Paul's gospel (Romans 5 on grace's super-abundance) is radical enough to attract the charge of moral permissiveness — which confirms the depth of grace's unconditionality. His answer (Romans 6:2-14) is grounded entirely in union with Christ, not in moral obligation as such.
2: A direct: "By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer?" Death-to-sin is not aspirational language but a description of a status change that occurred in union with Christ's death. The rhetorical force is: if you died to sin in Christ, continuing to live in it is ontologically impossible, not merely morally inadvisable.
3: A direct: "Or don't you know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?" Baptism as union with Christ's death establishes the basis of Romans 6:2 and 4. The death Paul is invoking is not metaphorical: it is participation in the historical death of Jesus, appropriated through faith and symbolized in baptism. The believer's identity is now defined by Christ's death.
4: A direct: "We were therefore buried with him through baptism into death in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may live a new life." The resurrection of Christ is not merely the believer's future hope but the pattern and power of present new life. The logic of Romans 4's resurrection-God is applied here: the God who raised Christ works in believers the same new-creation power.
5: A direct: "For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we will certainly also be united with him in a resurrection like his." The Greek symphytos (G4854, united with/grown together) is a biological term for organisms that have been grafted together; the union is organic, not merely legal. The certainty of the future resurrection grounds the present death-to-sin.
6: A direct: "For we know that our old self was crucified with him so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin." The "old self" (palaios anthrōpos) is the Adamic identity; its crucifixion with Christ is the decisive end of the sin-dominion established in Romans 5. This is not progressive sanctification but a definitive event accomplished in Christ's death.
7: A theme: "Because anyone who has died has been set free from sin." The axiom: death terminates the claims of a prior domain. As a deceased person cannot be claimed by their former employer, so the one who has died with Christ is released from sin's legal hold. The resurrection of Christ confirms that the release from sin's dominion is permanent.
8: A direct: "Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with him." The co-death establishes the co-life; they cannot be separated. The future "will live with him" encompasses both the present spiritual life and the future bodily resurrection, both grounded in the same union with Christ.
9: A direct: "For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, he cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over him." Christ's resurrection is permanent and unrepeatable: death's dominion over him is definitively ended. The resurrection is not resuscitation (return to the previous life) but transformation into a mode of existence no longer subject to death.
10: A direct: "The death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God." The adverb ephapax (once for all, G2178) marks the unrepeatable character of Christ's death to sin; its once-for-all nature is the ground for its universal sufficiency. The life he now lives is wholly oriented toward God — which is the pattern for the believer's own ethical life (Romans 6:11).
11: A direct: "In the same way, count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus." The imperative logizesthe (count/reckon, the imputation-verb from Romans 4) is applied here to personal identity: believers are to reckon their death and life according to their union with Christ rather than according to sin's claim. The status is established; the reckoning brings behavior into conformity with reality.
12: A theme: "Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its evil desires." The exhortation follows from the indicative (Romans 6:6-11); sin's reign has been broken in principle, and the practical realization of that break is the ethical task. "Mortal body" holds the tension: the body is still mortal (not yet resurrected) but need not be sin's instrument.
13: A direct: "Do not offer any part of yourself to sin as an instrument of wickedness, but rather offer yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life." The phrase "brought from death to life" is resurrection language applied to present existence; the transfer from death-domain to life-domain has occurred (Romans 6:4), and the ethical consequence is to present oneself to God in keeping with that transfer.
14: A direct: "For sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace." The kur- (master) language: sin's dominion is ended not by effort but by a change of covenant domain. Being under grace rather than law means standing in the sphere of Christ's accomplished work; sin's claim has no legal standing in that domain.
15: A theme: "What then? Shall we sin because we are not under the law but under grace?" The second antinomian objection is similar to the first (Romans 6:1) but focuses on grace's freedom from law rather than grace's super-abundance. The grace Paul preaches is radical enough that misunderstanding it as license is understandable — which is evidence of the gospel's genuine unconditionality.
16: A theme: "Don't you know that when you offer yourselves to someone as obedient slaves, you are slaves of the one you obey — whether you are slaves to sin, which leads to death, or to obedience, which leads to righteousness?" The slave-and-master binary replaces the law-and-grace binary of the previous argument; total allegiance to one master is the condition of the human will, and the transfer from sin's ownership to Christ's is the gospel's accomplishment.
17: A direct: "Thanks be to God that, though you used to be slaves to sin, you have come to obey from your heart the pattern of teaching that has now claimed your allegiance." The thanks is to God because the transfer of allegiance was God's act, not the believer's. "Pattern of teaching" (typos didachēs) is the apostolic deposit about Christ that reshapes the whole person.
18: A direct: "You have been set free from sin and have become slaves to righteousness." The passive eleutherōthentes (you were freed) indicates divine action; the active edoulōthēte (you became enslaved) indicates the resulting new allegiance. Freedom from sin and slavery to righteousness are the two sides of the same liberation that Christ's atoning death accomplished.
19: A theme: Paul uses human analogies about slavery "because of your human limitations." The apology for the metaphor recognizes its limitations; what he is describing (the complete reorientation of the will through Christ's work) is larger than any human analogy can contain. The direction is clear: toward holiness, which is the goal of the union with Christ Paul has been describing.
20: A theme: "When you were slaves to sin, you were free from the control of righteousness." The previous freedom from righteousness is exposed as slavery; what looked like liberty was servitude. Christ's liberation reverses this: freedom from sin produces willing slavery to the one whose service is perfect freedom.
21: A theme: "What benefit did you reap at that time from the things you are now ashamed of? Those things result in death!" The shame and death produced by the former life contrast with the holiness and eternal life of the new. The question exposes the bankruptcy of the old life by its own outcome — which is the argument by consequences that Paul uses to ground the new life in Christ.
22: A direct: "But now that you have been set free from sin and have become slaves of God, the benefit you reap leads to holiness, and the result is eternal life." The Christological logic: freedom from sin (Christ's atoning work) produces willing service to God (the Spirit's work) that leads to holiness (progressive) and eternal life (eschatological). The whole is Christ's doing, not the believer's achievement.
23: A direct: "For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." The final verse of the chapter draws the absolute contrast. Sin operates on wages-logic: it pays out what is earned. God operates on gift-logic: eternal life is given, not earned. The gift is specifically located "in Christ Jesus our Lord" — not as a general divine benevolence but as the fruit of the specific person and work of the Lord Jesus.
